content.full_text:
CRAIG S.J. JOHNS
1999 Harrison Street
Oakland, California 94612
(510) 763-2000


December 21, 1992


Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
Legal Division
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95812

Re:    Request for Formal Advice Regarding Potential Conflict of Interests

Dear Mr. Wallace:

     I am writing to request formal advice pursuant to California Government Code Section 83114(b) and 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18329(b) regarding my duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (Cal. Gov't. Code § 81000 et. seq. and 2 Cal. Code Regs. § 18700 et. seq.). I am a Member of the California Regional Water Quality Control Board, San Francisco Bay Region ("Regional Board"). This letter contains all facts material to my inquiry.

     On the agenda before the Regional Board for the month of December 1992 and January 1993 is a Proposed Amendment to the Water Quality Control Plan setting effluent limitations for selenium in San Francisco Bay ("Proposed Selenium Amendment"). I have enclosed, for your information, a draft copy of the Proposed Selenium Amendment that will be presented to the Regional Board in December and January, along with the staff report on the item.

     My wife owns, as her separate property bought prior to our marriage, common stock in Chevron U.S.A. valued at more than $1,000 but less than $10,000. All of the dividend income from my wife's investment is directly reinvested in Chevron common stock. I am informed that Chevron is a business entity listed on the New York Stock Exchange and is also listed on the Fortune Magazine Directory of the 500 largest U.S. industrial corporations.

     The Proposed Selenium Amendment is a revision to the San Francisco Region Basin Plan and, as such, is similar in nature to a general planning document. If adopted, the Proposed Selenium Amendment would apply to any party discharging effluent to San Francisco Bay. Nevertheless, I am informed by staff and legal counsel at the Regional Board that the impact of the Proposed Selenium Amendment will primarily affect oil refineries in the Bay Area in a

Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 21, 1992
Page 2


manner that is distinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect
the public generally, as set forth in Title 2 of California Code of Regulations
Section 18703 (hereinafter, "Regulations"). According to the staff report for the
Proposed Selenium Amendment, oil refineries reportedly account for between 56 -
70% of the total selenium discharged into San Francisco Bay.

The Proposed Selenium Amendment does not presently involve an
amendment, revision or modification of Chevron's existing permits issued by the
Regional Board, nor is Chevron a "named party" in the proceeding in which the
Proposed Selenium Amendment will be heard as contemplated in Regulations
Section 18702.1(b)(2). However, it is probable that, if the Proposed Selenium
Amendment is adopted, Chevron's permits may need to be revised in the future.
I am informed that it is not reasonably foreseeable at this time when Chevron's
permits may require revision if the Proposed Selenium Amendment is adopted.

I have requested information from Chevron about potential economic
impacts to the company if the Proposed Selenium Amendment is adopted.
According to the information provided by Chevron in the attached letter from Mr.
Jerry Rose, the Proposed Selenium Amendment, if adopted, would result in no
immediate increase or decrease to the gross revenues of Chevron in a fiscal year
totalling $1,000,000 or more. Moreover, it does not appear reasonably foreseeable
that Chevron would incur or avoid additional expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $250,000 or more. Depending on how the Regional Board chooses to
implement the Proposed Selenium Amendment, it is possible that Chevron may
incur expenses in excess of $250,000 or more in a fiscal year in order to comply
with future selenium discharge limitations. Nevertheless, Chevron maintains
that assessing the financial impacts of the Proposed Selenium Amendment in
future years is highly speculative.

I have three questions. First, based on my indirect financial interest in
Chevron and the economic effect of the Proposed Selenium Amendment on
Chevron as discussed above, do I have a "financial interest" in the Proposed
Selenium Amendment as contemplated under Government Code Section 87103
requiring my recusal on the matter? Second, is Chevron "directly" involved in
the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated in Regulations Section
18702.2(b)? Third, if my wife disposes of her investment in Chevron, by gift or
otherwise, to someone other than a member of my direct family, on what date
would my indirect financial interest in Chevron dissipate?

The Proposed Selenium Amendment is scheduled to be heard by the
Regional Board beginning on December 16, 1992 and will likely be continued to
the January 21, 1993 meeting. Therefore, I would greatly appreciate your
guidance as soon as possible. I appreciate your attention to and assistance with

Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
December 21, 1992
Page 3


this matter. Prior to issuing your formal advice letter, I would appreciate the
opportunity to speak with you if you have any questions concerning the factual
background or other elements of this request.

Very truly yours,

Craig S.J. Johns

CSJJbe
Enclosures

cc:  Gary Grimm, Esq.
     William Attwater, Esq.
     Steve Ritchie

sections.question: First, based on my indirect financial interest in Chevron and the economic effect of the Proposed Selenium Amendment on Chevron as discussed above, do I have a "financial interest" in the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated under Government Code Section 87103 requiring my recusal on the matter? Second, is Chevron "directly" involved in the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated in Regulations Section 18702.2(b)? Third, if my wife disposes of her investment in Chevron, by gift or otherwise, to someone other than a member of my direct family, on what date would my indirect financial interest in Chevron dissipate?
sections.question_synthetic: A California Regional Water Quality Control Board member seeks advice on whether his wife's stock ownership in Chevron creates a disqualifying financial interest in a proposed amendment regulating selenium discharge that would primarily affect oil refineries including Chevron, and asks when such interest would terminate if his wife divests the stock.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No conclusion or answer section is present. The document clearly contains three distinct questions from the requestor. Document appears complete with proper letterhead and signatures indicated.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: First, based on my indirect financial interest in Chevron and the economic effect of the Proposed Selenium Amendment on Chevron as discussed above, do I have a "financial interest" in the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated under Government Code Section 87103 requiring my recusal on the matter? Second, is Chevron "directly" involved in the Proposed Selenium Amendment as contemplated in Regulations Section 18702.2(b)? Third, if my wife disposes of her investment in Chevron, by gift or otherwise, to someone other than a member of my direct family, on what date would my indirect financial interest in Chevron dissipate?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request from a California Regional Water Quality Control Board member dated December 21, 1992, requesting formal FPPC advice regarding potential conflicts of interest arising from his wife's ownership of Chevron stock in connection with a proposed selenium discharge limitation amendment that would primarily affect oil refineries.
